nativeRange.habitat: Sandy and muddy substrates in open water and along the shoreline of Lake Malawi; often found lying motionless on the bottom in shallow to mid-depth zones

decorPreferences: large open sandy substrate for ambush behaviour
scattered rocks and cave formations around the perimeter
open midwater swimming space in the centre of the tank
minimal dense planting — robust species such as Vallisneria tolerated

diet.notes: An ambush piscivore in the wild; lies motionless on the substrate feigning death to lure smaller fish within striking range. In the aquarium, accepts large carnivore pellets, frozen and fresh whole fish (lancefish, smelt), krill, large earthworms, and meaty frozen foods (mussels, prawn). Live feeder fish are unnecessary and not recommended due to disease risk. Avoid high-plant-matter foods and beef heart — high fat content causes digestive and renal issues in African haplochromines. Feed once daily.

goodWith: large Lake Malawi haplochromines (Sciaenochromis, Copadichromis, Placidochromis)
large Nimbochromis species of similar size
large Lake Malawi peacock cichlids (Aulonocara spp.) of comparable size
avoidWith: small fish (any species under ~10 cm will be eaten)
mbuna (aggressive rockdwellers may harass and outcompete)
species requiring soft or acidic water

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Nimbochromis venustus; shows adult male in breeding colour (blue head, yellow-blotched body). Verify availability before selection.

summary: The Venustus or Giraffe Cichlid is a large Lake Malawi haplochromine renowned for its unique ambush-predator strategy — it lies motionless on the substrate to lure smaller fish. Adult males develop a vivid blue head and yellow giraffe-blotched body; juveniles are drab brown-blotched. Needs a 400 L+ tank with open sandy areas and hard alkaline water.
careNotes:
Nimbochromis venustus is one of the most visually striking Lake Malawi haplochromines, but also one of the largest and most predatory. A tank of at least 400 L with a 150 cm footprint is required for a single specimen or a small group; larger is always preferable. Water chemistry must reflect Lake Malawi conditions: hard (gH 10–20), alkaline (pH 7.6–8.6), and warm (24–28°C). Regular partial water changes of 25–30% weekly are essential to manage nitrates — this species is intolerant of poor water quality.

The hallmark behaviour of N. venustus is tonic immobility-based ambush predation: the fish tilts to its side and lies motionless on the substrate, resembling a dead fish, then strikes explosively at smaller fish that approach to investigate. This behaviour is exhibited even in captivity. Any tankmate under roughly 10 cm is at serious risk of predation, and feeders should never be used.

Colour is sex- and age-dependent. Juveniles of both sexes display brown bodies with dark blotches (the giraffe pattern). As males mature they develop the characteristic metallic-blue head and iridescent yellow flanks with retained brown-black blotching; females and subdominant males retain the juvenile pattern. This dimorphism means fish sold as juveniles may not show their adult colours for 18–24 months.

Compatible tankmates are limited to large (10 cm+) non-predatory haplochromines and Aulonocara peacocks of similar size, and robust Synodontis catfish. Mbuna are generally not suitable companions — their higher activity and aggression can prevent the Venustus from feeding effectively, and the two groups occupy different biotopes in the wild. Keep one male per tank unless the tank is very large (600 L+), as males are intensely territorial toward conspecifics and similar-looking species.

Do not feed beef heart or other mammalian proteins — these are linked to Malawi bloat (internal bacterial/protozoan infection, often fatal) in haplochromines. Feed a quality large cichlid carnivore pellet as the staple, supplemented with frozen krill, prawn, and occasional whole lancefish.
breedingNotes:
Nimbochromis venustus is a maternal mouthbrooder. Spawning follows typical Malawi haplochromine lekking behaviour — the male excavates a spawning pit on the sandy substrate, adopts full breeding colour, and courts females with spread fins and body shimmering. The female deposits eggs and immediately takes them into her mouth; she then mouths the male’s egg-spot markings on his anal fin, picking up sperm in the process and fertilising the clutch orally.

Clutch sizes typically range from 30 to 80 eggs depending on the female’s size and condition. The female holds the eggs and wrigglers in her buccal cavity for approximately 21–28 days, during which she does not feed. She should be moved to a dedicated holding tank or provided with dense cover to prevent harassment. After release, fry are large enough to accept crushed cichlid pellets and micro-worms immediately. The female may take fry back into her mouth when threatened for another week or so post-release.

Breeding in captivity is achievable at intermediate level: a harem of one male and two to three females is the most reliable approach, with the male kept in good condition on a varied meaty diet. Minimum recommended breeding tank size is 400 L.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Nimbochromis venustus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 25 cm), temperature (24–28°C), pH (7.5–8.5), hardness, native range (Lake Malawi), biotope, ambush predator behaviour notes, diet, compatibility, and breeding details including maternal mouthbrooding and clutch size.

additional.name: FishBase — Nimbochromis venustus
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum recorded adult size (25 cm TL), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Perciformes), native range (Lake Malawi endemic, Malawi and Mozambique), depth range, and diet classification. Confirmed piscivore/carnivore diet type.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — African Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: Supplementary source for general Lake Malawi haplochromine husbandry: water parameter ranges (pH 7.5–8.5, hardness, temperature), tank size guidance, diet cautions (no beef heart, Malawi bloat risk), and compatbility framework for non-mbuna Malawi cichlids.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Nimbochromis venustus profile
additional.notes: Supplementary trade-press source confirming ambush predator strategy (tonic immobility / playing dead), juvenile vs adult colouration difference, minimum tank size recommendation (~450 L), and mouthbrooding details including female holding period (~3 weeks) and fry size at release.

additional.name: Cichlid-Forum — Nimbochromis venustus species entry
additional.notes: Community hobbyist reference cross-checking tank size (minimum 6-foot tank / ~400 L), conspecific aggression (males intensely territorial), compatible tankmates list, and confirmation that juveniles sold in the trade are predominantly drab brown before adult colouration develops at 18–24 months.

fish.breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder. Female incubates 30–80 eggs for 21–28 days without feeding. Harem setup (1M:2–3F) recommended. Fry accept crushed pellets and micro-worms immediately on release. Move holding female to a separate tank to prevent harassment.
